vectors.name: Inc Procedure
vectors.syntax: procedure Inc(var X: Ordinal [; N: Longint]);
vectors.description: Increments an ordinal variable. If N is specified, increments by N.

quirks_and_tests.description: Like Delete and Insert, Inc is a mutating procedure, not a function. It operates directly on the memory address of the variable. It works on any ordinal type (Integer, Char, Boolean).

vectors.name: Dec Procedure
vectors.syntax: procedure Dec(var X: Ordinal [; N: Longint]);
vectors.description: Decrements an ordinal variable. If N is specified, decrements by N.

quirks_and_tests.description: The inverse of Inc. Mutates the variable in place. Highly optimized by the compiler into native DEC or SUB assembly instructions.

vectors.name: Succ and Pred Functions
vectors.syntax:
function Succ(X: Ordinal): Ordinal;
function Pred(X: Ordinal): Ordinal;
vectors.description: Returns the successor or predecessor of an ordinal value.

quirks_and_tests.description: Unlike Inc and Dec, these are purely functional and do not mutate the argument. They evaluate to the next or previous value in the ordinal sequence.

vectors.name: Ord Function
vectors.description: Returns the ordinal value (memory integer representation) of an ordinal-type expression.

quirks_and_tests.description: Standard ASCII mapping for characters. Structurally tied to Pascal's strict ordinal type system.

vectors.name: Chr Function
vectors.description: Returns the character corresponding to a specific ASCII value.

quirks_and_tests.description: Specifically expects a Byte (0-255). It maps directly to the character in the extended ASCII memory table.

vectors.name: SizeOf Function
vectors.description: Returns the number of bytes occupied by a variable or a type.

quirks_and_tests.description: CRITICAL QUIRK: Memory footprint verification. In 16-bit MS-DOS, an Integer is 2 bytes, a Longint is 4 bytes, a standard String is 256 bytes (Length byte + 255 chars). Crucially, the default Real type is Borland's proprietary 'Real48' format, which occupies exactly 6 bytes (unlike IEEE 754 8-byte Doubles).
